name: @chbrain/khai-engine-guilt
description: khai engine content, the guilt domain: guilt as process, how a persona appraises a specific act against a standard it holds as its own, and the appraisal becomes a reparative pull toward confession, apology, or amends.
license: SEE LICENSE IN LICENSE and LICENSE-CODE

khai.tagline: Guilt as process: how a persona appraises a specific act against a standard it holds as its own, and the appraisal becomes a reparative pull -- confess it, own it, make amends -- rather than a verdict on the whole self.

card.wire: Attaches at Process. The root (process_guilt.md) declares guilt as an act-focused appraisal: a chosen act meets a standard the persona holds as its own, and the read becomes a reparative pull rather than a verdict on the whole self.
card.issue: The appraisal, read three ways beneath the root. One process names the crossing itself (transgression: the act against the standard, committed and irreversible); one position names the stance it leaves the persona holding (culpable: author of record, account open); one process names the way the account closes (reparation: confession, apology, restitution delivered to the party or standard it is owed).
card.require: Two altitudes hold the engine. The law: declared once in the project's Instructions Knowledge chapter, linking the root. The link: each persona links, under Projection, the specific reading it runs in the scene.
card.enforce: Structure is checked; coherence is judged. The kit verifies the floor: the law is declared and each persona links a real guilt reading. Whether the reading is true to the persona's act is semantic, taught and reviewed, never failed by the suite.
card.setup: Declare the law in the Instructions Knowledge chapter, linking the root. Then give the persona its read under Projection, linking the specific expression -- the transgression it committed, the culpable stance it holds, or the reparation it is attempting. Read it in the pull toward repair, never as a narrated verdict on who the persona is.
